title: Unconventional incommensurate epitaxy of superconducting FeSe films on SrTiO$_3$
authors: M. Klement, K. M. Fijalkowski, M. Kamp, C. Gould, L. W. Molenkamp
abstract: We present a combined X-ray diffraction and transmission electron microscopy study of superconducting FeSe/FeTe multilayers grown by molecular beam epitaxy on SrTiO$_3$(001) substrates. While X-ray diffraction confirms perfect in-plane epitaxial alignment between FeSe, FeTe, and the substrate, scanning transmission electron microscopy reveals a surprising lack of atomic registry at the FeSe/SrTiO$_3$ interface. Instead of adapting to the substrate lattice, FeSe retains its own in-plane lattice spacing. A periodic lateral shift between the atomic positions of FeSe and SrTiO$_3$ is observed, with a registry recurrence length that matches the lattice mismatch determined by X-ray diffraction. No misfit dislocations or other relaxation features are detected at the interface. This coexistence of directional alignment and registry-free growth suggests an unconventional regime of epitaxy in which crystallographic orientation is maintained without atomic matching. The findings offer insight into strain accommodation in layered systems and may have implications for interface engineering in Fe-based superconductors.
summary: This work reports on superconducting FeSe/FeTe films grown on SrTiO3, demonstrating an unusual epitaxial growth mode. Using combined XRD and STEM, the authors prove that the films maintain their intrinsic lattice spacing despite a significant mismatch with the substrate. This 'registry-free' epitaxy challenges conventional understanding of strain accommodation in layered superconductors.
main_problem: The study investigates how superconducting FeSe/FeTe multilayers can maintain perfect in-plane epitaxial alignment on a mismatched SrTiO3 substrate without forming expected structural defects like misfit dislocations.
main_result: The films exhibit an unconventional, registry-free epitaxy where the intrinsic lattice spacing of the deposited materials is preserved despite a significant mismatch with the substrate. This suggests directional alignment can occur independently of atomic matching.
method: The research combines X-ray Diffraction (XRD) to determine bulk and in-plane lattice parameters, with high-resolution Scanning Transmission Electron Microscopy (STEM) and Geometric Phase Analysis (GPA) to probe atomic interfaces at the nanoscale.
model_or_system: The physical system is a multilayer stack of superconducting FeSe/FeTe films grown epitaxially on SrTiO3(001) substrates using Molecular Beam Epitaxy. The analysis focuses on the structural interface between these crystalline materials.
key_observables: In-plane lattice constants (e.g., $a_{	ext{FeSe}} = 3.862 \mathring{	ext{A}}$), absence of misfit dislocations, and periodic lateral shifts observed via STEM/GPA.
important_parameters: Lattice mismatch ($\sim 1.1\%$), superconducting transition temperature ($T_c < 14	ext{ K}$), and the characteristic length scale of registry recurrence (e.g., $\lambda \sim 30 	ext{ nm}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the observed structural continuity implies a specific, non-conventional epitaxial growth mechanism that bypasses standard strain relaxation pathways.
figures_summary: Figures show optical micrographs of devices, R-T curves confirming superconductivity, and high-resolution STEM cross-sections illustrating atomically sharp interfaces with no visible dislocations or lattice mismatch accommodation features.
paper_structure: The paper proceeds by first establishing the superconducting nature of the films using transport measurements. It then uses XRD to confirm perfect in-plane alignment and measure lattice parameters. Finally, detailed STEM analysis is used to visualize the interface structure, confirming the registry-free growth mode across multiple nanometers.
why_interesting: While primarily condensed matter experimental physics, the concept of maintaining long-range order (crystallographic orientation) without local atomic matching presents a fascinating analogy for understanding boundary conditions or coupling mechanisms in quantum systems where perfect commensurability is not required.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a: Optical micrograph of the eight-terminal device geometry used for resistance measurements. b: Four-terminal resistance as a function of temperature, showing a clear su- perconducting transition in a nominally identical FeSe/FeTe sample. Solid markers with error bars are the measured data; the smooth curve is only a guide to the eye to highlight the overall transition proﬁle. The inset shows a zoomed-in seg- ment of the data near zero resistance (note the units here are milliohms).

FIG. 2. ω-2θ scan of a FeSe thin ﬁlm capped with FeTe, grown on a SrTiO3(001) substrate. Reﬂections from the sub- strate, FeSe, and FeTe are clearly visible. Pronounced Kiessig fringes appear as closely spaced oscillations around the FeTe reﬂections. Weaker and more widely spaced Kiessig oscilla- tions can also be discerned around the FeSe(001) reﬂection at approximately 18.5◦, consistent with the thinner FeSe layer. A high-resolution view of the FeTe(001) reﬂection, highlight- ing the surrounding Kiessig oscillations, is shown in Fig. 3, where the Kiessig oscillations are indicated by arrows.

FIG. 3. a: Kiessig fringes at the FeTe(001) reﬂection (indi- cated by arrows) used to extract a thickness of 20.8 nm. b: Rocking curve of the FeSe(003) peak showing a FWHM of 0.036◦(130 arcsec), indicating exceptional crystalline quality and alignment.

FIG. 5. High-resolution HAADF-STEM images of the FeSe/SrTiO3 heterostructure. (a) Overview of the full stack, showing the SrTiO3 substrate, a TiO2 double layer, epitaxial FeSe and FeTe ﬁlms, and the amorphous tellurium capping layer. (b) Magniﬁed view of the FeSe/SrTiO3 interface. Ver- tical red lines mark selected unit cell positions in both SrTiO3 and FeSe along the in-plane direction. A progressive lateral shift of the FeSe lattice is observed, reaching a half-unit-cell oﬀset after approximately 30 unit cells and full registry af- ter approximately 60. The TiO2 double layer is clearly re- solved at the interface. (c) Geometric phase analysis (GPA) map derived from panel (b), showing the...

FIG. 4. Azimuthal φ-scans of the (112) reﬂections from SrTiO3 (blue), FeSe (red), and FeTe (black). All three scans exhibit a clear fourfold symmetry with peaks spaced by 90°, indicating a shared in-plane crystallographic orientation. The peak positions coincide at φ = 45◦, 135◦, 225◦, and 315◦, con- ﬁrming an untwinned, orientation-coherent in-plane epitaxial relationship between the ﬁlm layers and the substrate. Verti- cal oﬀsets have been applied for clarity.
